metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan (or AC4) the minimum required (gate × scope) cross-product — e.g. 'plan-review gate: systemic only; completion gate: systemic + per-criterion; code-review gate: systemic only' — so an executor knows exactly which combinations are in scope without clarifying.
findings.finding: 'Two-scope (systemic vs per-criterion) and force/disabled guard scenarios are covered' (AC4) does not state WHICH gate types require both scopes. The Scope section names three gates (plan-review, completion, code-review) but does not specify whether the per-criterion scope must be exercised for all three, a representative subset, or only one. An executor choosing 'one gate for per-criterion' and 'one gate for systemic' may omit coverage the author considers essential.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 'Two-scope (systemic vs per-criterion) and force/disabled guard scenarios are covered'; Scope section — 'Two-scope scenarios: systemic (whole op) vs per-criterion (one unit among N)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `ContentFilterError` as a distinct injectable exception class from the failure matrix and replace it with a `FunctionModel` that returns a canned response with `finish_reason='content_filter'` (matching how runner.py and structured.py actually surface this condition), noting this exercises `structured.py:219`'s `_BAD_STOP_REASONS` path rather than an exception raise.
findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is listed in the failure matrix as a distinct injectable exception class, but no such symbol exists anywhere in the rebar codebase or in pydantic-ai's exception surface (only `ModelHTTPError` and `UsageLimitExceeded` are imported from `pydantic_ai.exceptions`). The `content_filter` signal is implemented solely as a `finish_reason` string value in `structured.py:219`, not as a raiseable exception. The plan's 'symbol-presence assertion at collection time' requirement would therefore cause a hard `ImportError`/`AttributeError` at collection, breaking the entire test file.
findings.location: Scope section: 'ContentFilterError' in failure matrix; AC1: 'Each pydantic-ai symbol asserted to exist as imported'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `ContentFilterError` entry with a `content_filter` finish-reason injection case: a `FunctionModel` returning a `ModelResponse` with `finish_reason='content_filter'`, asserting `UnretryableOutputError` (structured.py:229) is raised and gate behavior is INDETERMINATE.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `IncompleteToolCall` (or an equivalent pydantic-ai symbol for incomplete/partial tool calls) actually exists in the installed pydantic-ai 1.107.0 SDK; if absent, remove it from the failure matrix or replace it with the correct symbol/mechanism (e.g. a malformed tool-call response injected via `FunctionModel` returning a partial `ToolCallPart`).
findings.finding: `IncompleteToolCall` is named as a discrete failure-matrix entry in the Scope section, but this symbol does not exist anywhere in the rebar codebase (src/ or tests/) and produces zero search hits. It is not a known public symbol in pydantic-ai's exception or message surface. The plan's collection-time symbol-presence assertion requirement means an attempted import would raise `ImportError`, breaking the test file.
findings.location: Scope section: 'IncompleteToolCall' in the failure matrix list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `ContentFilterError` is an importable pydantic-ai exception class or solely a `finish_reason` string value — if the latter, remove it from the symbol-presence assertion list and document it as a canned `finish_reason='content_filter'` FunctionModel fixture instead.
findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is listed in the Scope failure matrix as a distinct injectable exception class ('ContentFilterError' bullet), but this symbol does not exist in `pydantic_ai.exceptions` or anywhere in src/ or tests/. The content-filter case in pydantic-ai is a `finish_reason='content_filter'` string, not a raised exception. The plan's own AC states 'Each pydantic-ai symbol asserted to exist as imported … a renamed/absent SDK symbol fails loudly rather than silently skips' — meaning a `from pydantic_ai.exceptions import ContentFilterError` would cause test collection to fail immediately.
findings.location: Scope — failure matrix bullet: '`content_filter` finish_reason, `ContentFilterError`'

Remove any `from pydantic_ai.exceptions import ContentFilterError` collection-time assertion.

findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is named in the failure matrix as a pydantic-ai symbol that must be import-asserted at collection time, but this class does not exist in pydantic-ai's public exception surface (only `ModelHTTPError` and `UsageLimitExceeded` are real symbols in `pydantic_ai.exceptions`). findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped' claim: verify that timeout failure-matrix test cases assert `LLMUnavailableError` (runner.py:365) as the current behavior, not the bare `httpx.ReadTimeout`, because runner.py:353's broad `except Exception` wraps all non-LLMError exceptions.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped' as a validated fact, but the runner's broad `except Exception` at runner.py:353–365 catches ALL exceptions that are not `UsageLimitExceeded` or `LLMError` subclasses — including `httpx.ReadTimeout` — and re-raises them wrapped in `LLMUnavailableError`. The test suite must therefore assert `LLMUnavailableError` (not bare `httpx.ReadTimeout`) for the timeout failure classes, or the RED test will be written against an incorrect current-behavior premise.
findings.location: Why section: 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped'; Scope: 'httpx.ConnectTimeout', 'httpx.ReadTimeout' failure-matrix entries
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why section and in the test fixture comments, replace 'propagates unwrapped' with 'wrapped into LLMUnavailableError by runner.py:353'. The CURRENT inline comment on those test cases should read: `# CURRENT(runner.py:365): httpx.ReadTimeout wrapped into LLMUnavailableError`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `httpx.ReadTimeout` / `httpx.ConnectTimeout` test assertions to match today's actual runner behavior: both are caught by `runner.py:353` and re-raised as `LLMUnavailableError`; the `# CURRENT(runner.py:353)` comment should document this wrapping, and `pytest.raises(LLMUnavailableError)` is the correct assertion (with `match` on the original error type in the message if desired).
findings.finding: The plan states `httpx.ReadTimeout` 'propagates unwrapped' (from the brainstorm section), which implies the test should assert the caller receives a raw `httpx.ReadTimeout`. But `runner.py:353` is a bare `except Exception` that catches ALL non-`UsageLimitExceeded`, non-`LLMError` exceptions — including `httpx.ReadTimeout` — and re-raises them as `LLMUnavailableError`. A test asserting the unwrapped `httpx.ReadTimeout` would go RED on today's code, violating AC #1.
findings.location: Why section: 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped'; Scope / failure matrix: 'httpx.ConnectTimeout, httpx.ReadTimeout'
findings.suggested_fix: In the parametrized case for httpx timeout failures, assert `pytest.raises(LLMUnavailableError)` and add `# CURRENT(runner.py:353): httpx.ReadTimeout/ConnectTimeout wrapped into LLMUnavailableError by the broad except Exception clause`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the 'propagates unwrapped' claim: verify whether it refers to the raw pydantic-ai layer (before the runner catches it) or the caller-visible behavior; update the matrix description and test assertions to match the actual runner behavior — httpx.ReadTimeout injected via FunctionModel is caught by runner.py:353-365 and re-raised as `LLMUnavailableError`, so the test should assert `LLMUnavailableError` with `__cause__` being `httpx.ReadTimeout`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped' as a brainstorm-validated fact, but the runner's broad `except Exception` at runner.py:353-365 wraps ALL non-LLMError exceptions — including httpx.ReadTimeout — into LLMUnavailableError, so ReadTimeout does NOT propagate unwrapped to the test assertion layer.
findings.location: Why section — 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped'; Scope section — failure matrix, 'httpx.ConnectTimeout', 'httpx.ReadTimeout' entries
findings.suggested_fix: Assert `pytest.raises(LLMUnavailableError)` for both ConnectTimeout and ReadTimeout injections, and optionally add `assert isinstance(exc_info.value.__cause__, httpx.ReadTimeout)` to verify the original exception is preserved as the cause.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Why' section claim: `httpx.ReadTimeout` does NOT propagate unwrapped through the runner — it is caught by runner.py:353-365's broad `except Exception` and re-raised as `LLMUnavailableError`; the RED test cases for httpx timeout failure classes must assert `LLMUnavailableError`, not the raw httpx exception.
findings.finding: The 'Why' section asserts 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped' as a brainstorm-validated fact. This is contradicted by runner.py:353-365, which has a broad `except Exception` that catches ALL non-LLMError exceptions — including `httpx.ReadTimeout` — and wraps them into `LLMUnavailableError`. The RED test cases for httpx.ConnectTimeout and httpx.ReadTimeout should therefore assert `LLMUnavailableError` (consistent with the Scope's 'everything collapses to LLMUnavailableError'), not that the httpx exception propagates unwrapped. The inaccurate 'Why' claim risks the implementer writing the wrong assertion.
findings.location: Why section — 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the 'Why' sentence to: 'httpx.ReadTimeout raised inside a FunctionModel is caught by runner.py:353's broad except-Exception handler and re-raised as LLMUnavailableError — consistent with the Scope's statement that everything collapses to LLMUnavailableError today.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Why' section: `httpx.ReadTimeout` does NOT propagate unwrapped on today's code — it is caught by `runner.py:353` and re-raised as `LLMUnavailableError`; update the `httpx.ReadTimeout` failure-matrix entry's expected current behavior to `LLMUnavailableError` (with the original `httpx.ReadTimeout` accessible via `__cause__`).
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section asserts as a brainstorm-validated fact that '`httpx.ReadTimeout` propagates unwrapped', but the actual runner code at `runner.py:353-365` shows a broad `except Exception` that wraps ALL exceptions that are not `UsageLimitExceeded` or `LLMError` subclasses into `LLMUnavailableError`. Since `httpx.ReadTimeout` is not an `LLMError` subclass, it will be caught and re-raised as `LLMUnavailableError(...) from exc` — not unwrapped. The RED test that pins 'current behavior' would correctly assert `LLMUnavailableError`, but the plan's stated reason for believing this is the behavior contradicts the code, creating a structural ambiguity in what 'current behavior' the test should pin.
findings.location: Why section: 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped'; Scope section: '`httpx.ReadTimeout`' failure matrix bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped' to 'httpx.ReadTimeout is caught at runner.py:353 and re-raised as LLMUnavailableError (accessible via __cause__)'; update the failure-matrix bullet's pinned assertion accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped' as a brainstorm-validated fact, but the runner's broad `except Exception` at runner.py:353 catches ALL non-LLMError exceptions — including httpx.ReadTimeout — and wraps them in `LLMUnavailableError`. ReadTimeout does NOT propagate unwrapped; it arrives at callers as `LLMUnavailableError`. A test asserting unwrapped propagation will fail on current code.
findings.location: Scope section — 'httpx.ReadTimeout propagates unwrapped' / runner.py:351-365
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan's brainstorm note to: 'httpx.ReadTimeout is caught by the broad except Exception and surfaces as LLMUnavailableError(... caused by ReadTimeout) — the test asserts isinstance(exc, LLMUnavailableError) and isinstance(exc.__cause__, httpx.ReadTimeout)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dedicated AC (or extend AC #4) to explicitly require liveness-scenario cases: a hung-model-request case and a hung-tool case that document today's absence of per-request/per-tool timeout, specifying their pass/xfail status under `make test`.
findings.finding: The Scope's 'Named liveness scenarios' (hung model request, hung tool, looping model reaching derived request_limit) are described as required coverage, but no AC explicitly names liveness scenarios. AC #4 covers 'Two-scope (systemic vs per-criterion) and force/disabled guard scenarios' — not liveness. AC #1 covers 'every failure class' but the failure matrix list does not include 'hung model request' or 'hung tool' as named entries, only `UsageLimitExceeded` / `tool_calls_limit` for the looping case.
findings.location: Scope section (Named liveness scenarios) vs AC #4 and failure matrix list

findings.finding: AC2 requires 'each case asserts the current runner-level and gate-level behavior with a source citation comment' but 'source citation comment' is effort-framed with no proving command — `make test` cannot verify that comments cite sources, and 'source citation' is not defined (file:line? docstring? inline comment?).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the liveness/hang RED tests: state whether the test (a) inspects runner/config attributes for absence of a timeout value (fast, weak), (b) uses a threading.Thread with a sentinel to verify the call does not return within N seconds and then cancels it (slow, strong), or (c) another named approach — and choose a default N if option (b) is selected.
findings.finding: The 'hung model' and 'hung tool' liveness scenarios assert 'the current unbounded/hanging behavior, e.g. via a short outer test-level guard,' but the mechanism for doing so without the test itself hanging indefinitely is unresolved. If the guard triggers (killing the hung call), the test passes by raising an exception — but that exception would look like a timeout error, not 'no timeout configured,' so it's unclear whether the assertion is (a) that the call never returns within N seconds, (b) that no internal timeout is set on the runner object, or (c) something else. An executor cannot choose between these interpretations without stopping to clarify.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Named liveness scenarios pin CURRENT (pre-change) behavior only: a hung model request and a hung tool document that TODAY there is no per-request/per-tool timeout (the RED test asserts the current unbounded/hanging behavior, e.g. via a short outer test-level guard)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'e.g. via a short outer test-level guard' with a stated default: 'Use a `threading.Thread` + `Event` to invoke the hanging FunctionModel; assert the thread is still alive after 0.5 s, then cancel it. This proves the runner issues no internal timeout. Do NOT use `pytest-timeout` or `signal.alarm` on these cases as they would cause the test to fail rather than pass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'asserting current unbounded/hanging behavior' (Scope) and 'make test green' (AC #1) by specifying the exact test pattern for hung scenarios — e.g., `@pytest.mark.xfail(reason='no timeout today', strict=True)` with a short guard, or a `threading.Event`-based mock that unblocks after N ms and asserts no result was produced.
findings.finding: The Scope section describes asserting 'current unbounded/hanging behavior' via 'a short outer test-level guard,' but AC #1 requires 'make test runs it green on TODAY's code.' A test that asserts unbounded/hanging behavior cannot pass green with a simple timeout guard — if the guard fires (test times out), the test fails; if it passes, no hang was observed. The plan does not specify the assertion mechanism (e.g., xfail+timeout, inverted assertion, skip-with-comment) that reconciles these two requirements, leaving the implementer with contradictory signals: the hung-model test must both be green AND document unbounded behavior.
findings.location: Scope section (Named liveness scenarios) vs AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope's Named liveness scenarios paragraph, add: 'The hung-model and hung-tool cases use a threading-based mock that blocks for a configurable delay; the test asserts the runner does not return within that delay using `pytest.raises(TimeoutError)` at the test level with `@pytest.mark.timeout(N)`, or alternatively are marked `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)` with a documented rationale comment. AC #1's green requirement is satisfied by the xfail/expected-failure pass status.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the 'hung model/tool' liveness scenarios: e.g., use `threading.Event` / `asyncio.Event` to synchronize that the runner is BLOCKED (not that it timed out), or document that these cases use `pytest-timeout` with a named marker and explicitly assert a `TimeoutError`/`pytest.fail` — whichever approach, name it in the plan and confirm it is deterministic under CI load.
findings.finding: The 'hung model request' and 'hung tool' liveness scenarios assert 'current unbounded/hanging behavior, e.g. via a short outer test-level guard' — but the mechanism is not specified. A timeout-based outer guard makes the assertion 'it does not complete within T seconds,' which is non-deterministic in slow CI environments and does not faithfully pin 'unbounded' behavior. Alternatively, without a guard, these cases hang the test suite. The plan needs to specify the concrete assertion mechanism for the hung-behavior cases.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Named liveness scenarios pin CURRENT behavior only: a hung model request and a hung tool document that TODAY there is no per-request/per-tool timeout (the RED test asserts the current unbounded/hanging behavior, e.g. via a short outer test-level guard)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'hung' behavioral assertion with a synchronization-based probe: inject a `FunctionModel` that sets a threading.Event (proving the runner entered the call) then blocks on a second Event that the test never releases; assert the Event was set (proving the runner reached the model layer) and then cancel/terminate the run — this pins 'runner reaches the model and blocks' without a wall-clock timeout and without hanging CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the hung-model liveness baseline test: either (a) mark with `@pytest.mark.timeout(N)` if `pytest-timeout` is a project dependency, (b) run the blocking call in a `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` with a short `wait(timeout=N)` and assert it did NOT complete within N seconds (proving no timeout fires today), or (c) skip this liveness case from this story and scope it only to the story that adds the per-request timeout — since pinning 'hangs forever' as a baseline is fragile.
findings.finding: The 'liveness' baseline scenario for a hung model request says it 'asserts the current unbounded/hanging behavior, e.g. via a short outer test-level guard,' but the plan does not specify the concrete mechanism to prevent the test from hanging pytest indefinitely. A `FunctionModel` that blocks forever will cause pytest to hang unless an explicit per-test timeout mechanism is used, but no such mechanism is named.
findings.location: Scope section: 'a hung model request and a hung tool document that TODAY there is no per-request/per-tool timeout (the RED test asserts the current unbounded/hanging behavior, e.g. via a short outer test-level guard)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope section a concrete injection recipe for finish_reason cases: specify which `pydantic_ai.messages` type (e.g. `ModelResponse` with a specific `finish_reason` field, or a `ModelResponse` subclass) a `FunctionModel` callback must return to exercise the `result.response.finish_reason` path at `runner.py:483`, citing the pydantic-ai 1.107.0 API.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'a canned `finish_reason` is readable at `result.response.finish_reason`' as a brainstorm-validated fact, but nowhere specifies the protocol for constructing a `FunctionModel` callback that actually populates this field. The runner accesses it via `getattr(result.response, 'finish_reason', None)` at `runner.py:483` — but `FunctionModel`'s callback receives `(messages, info)` and must return a `ModelResponse`. The plan does not define what `ModelResponse` fields or subclass to use, nor what value to pass, leaving an executor without a concrete injection recipe for the `content_filter`/`length`/`error` finish_reason cases.
findings.location: Why section: 'a canned finish_reason is readable at result.response.finish_reason'; Scope section: 'length/truncation', 'content_filter finish_reason' matrix entries

findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'everything collapses to `LLMUnavailableError` / `LLMRunnerError` today' at the runner level, but the runner at runner.py:351-352 has `except LLMError: raise` which passes `StructuredOutputError` and `UnretryableOutputError` through UNCHANGED. Validation-retry exhaustion (from `_pai_structured`) raises `StructuredOutputError` (a subclass of `LLMRunnerError` but distinct), and truncation raises `UnretryableOutputError`. These do NOT collapse to the two named types.
findings.location: What section — 'runner classification (everything collapses to LLMUnavailableError / LLMRunnerError today)' / runner.py:351-352, 486-499
findings.suggested_fix: Add a classification table to the plan: (a) network/auth/rate-limit/unknown → LLMUnavailableError; (b) step-budget exceeded (UsageLimitExceeded) → LLMRunnerError; (c) structured output parse/validate failure → StructuredOutputError; (d) truncation/content-filter finish_reason → UnretryableOutputError. Tests must assert the specific type, not a single collapsed type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether the `# CURRENT(file:line)` inline comment requirement can be enforced by a simpler means (e.g., a required `pytest.mark` or a per-case `source_pin` tuple in the parametrize data) that is consistent with existing codebase patterns, rather than a source-grepping meta-test with 0 prior art.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a meta-test that 'greps the test source for a `# CURRENT(` marker per parametrized id' — a bespoke self-inspection mechanism with zero existing call-sites in the codebase. This introduces a novel pattern (a test that reads its own source file via `Path(__file__)`) that has no Rule-of-Three grounding and rebuilds what could be achieved with a simpler convention (e.g., a required `marks` field on each parametrize entry, or a docstring invariant checked by a conftest plugin).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('its presence on every case is checked by a meta-test that greps the test source for a `# CURRENT(` marker per parametrized id')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC explicitly requiring that each pydantic-ai symbol in the failure matrix (e.g. `ModelHTTPError`, `UsageLimitExceeded`, `ContentFilterError`, etc.) is imported at module level in the test file (not inside test bodies), so a collection-time `ImportError` fails the suite loudly — and that this is verified by the meta-test or a dedicated import-sanity test.
findings.finding: The Scope describes a distinct deliverable — symbol-presence assertions at collection time ('Each pydantic-ai symbol asserted to exist as imported... a renamed/absent SDK symbol fails loudly rather than silently skips') — but no AC explicitly covers this mechanism. AC #1 covers 'exercises every failure class via model_override' but does not verify that symbol imports are checked at collection time as a standalone assertion layer.
findings.location: Scope section (failure matrix, first paragraph) vs AC #1–#5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <behavior>` annotation convention and the enforcing meta-test pattern in CLAUDE.md (or a `docs/test-conventions.md`) so future story authors can follow it without reverse-engineering the grep.
findings.finding: The `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>)` annotation convention and its enforcement meta-test constitute a new cross-story developer contract (future stories must follow this convention to flip tests green), but the plan names no navigable location (CLAUDE.md, a test-conventions doc, or contributing guide) where this convention is recorded for future story authors.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC bullet 2 ('carries an inline `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <one-line behavior>` comment … verified by code inspection … presence … checked by a meta-test that greps the test source')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to CLAUDE.md under a 'Test Conventions' heading: '**Failure-matrix baseline annotations**: every parametrized case in `tests/unit/test_llm_failure_matrix.py` must carry a `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <one-line behavior>` comment. A meta-test (`test_all_cases_have_current_marker`) greps the source for this marker and fails if any case omits it. Future stories that flip a case to new behavior replace the `# CURRENT(...)` comment with `# NEW(...)` (or delete it) and update the meta-test allowlist.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the lifecycle of `ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS=False`: define a scoped pytest fixture (autouse, function- or module-scoped) in the plan that sets the flag to `False` and restores it to its original value via `yield`/teardown, so the guard cannot bleed into other test modules.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS=False` as a test guard but does not define the lifecycle of this global pydantic-ai flag: who sets it, at what scope (session/module/function), and whether it is restored to its original value after the test run, risking contamination of other test modules sharing the same process.
findings.location: AC line 1 / What section: 'Guarded by `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False`'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the plan: 'An autouse module-scoped fixture in `test_llm_failure_matrix.py` saves `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS`, sets it to `False`, and restores it after the module via `yield` — e.g.:
```python
@pytest.fixture(autouse=True, scope="module")
def _block_model_requests():
    original = pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS
    pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False
    yield
    pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = original
```'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the 'guarded by `ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS=False`' AC refers to relying on the existing `tests/conftest.py` session-autouse fixture (no per-file re-set needed), and update the AC wording accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'Guarded by `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False`' as if the new test file must establish this guard, but `tests/conftest.py:247-260` already applies it as a `scope='session', autouse=True` fixture for the entire test suite. Adding a redundant per-file `ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False` statement rebuilds a protection already centrally provided.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('guarded by `ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS=False`'); What section; tests/conftest.py:247-260
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC1 to: 'relies on the existing `tests/conftest.py` session-autouse `_no_live_model_requests` fixture (no per-file re-set); add a `pytest.importorskip("pydantic_ai")` guard as the only required preamble.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider extracting the shared `FunctionModel`-based test helpers (fixed-text, sequence, raising, looping) into `tests/unit/conftest.py` or a `tests/unit/fixtures/llm_models.py` module, given that >=3 test files already define independent copies; the new failure-matrix file would then import from there rather than defining a 5th variant.
findings.finding: The `_function_model` and `_sequence_model` FunctionModel factory helpers are already independently defined in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:26-52`, `test_runner_hardening.py:23-36`, and implicitly in `test_runner_pydantic_budget.py` (inline looping FunctionModel). The new file will introduce a 4th set of similar helpers (raising FunctionModel, canned-finish-reason FunctionModel, etc.) without a shared conftest fixture, deepening the existing duplication. The Rule-of-Three threshold is already met across the 3+ existing files.
findings.location: What section ('A parametrized test file … exercising every failure class via model_override'); tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:26-52; tests/unit/test_runner_hardening.py:23-36; tests/unit/test_runner_pydantic_budget.py:29-39

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit the existing test files (test_runner_pydantic_budget.py, test_runner_hardening.py, test_pydantic_ai_runner.py, test_agentic_budget_and_truncation.py) against the proposed failure matrix and explicitly state which cases are net-new vs already covered, and whether the new file subsumes or supplements those files.
findings.finding: The plan proposes substantial new test infrastructure (parametrized failure matrix, symbol-presence meta-test, # CURRENT annotation convention) but does not consider whether the existing prior-art test files (test_runner_pydantic_budget.py, test_runner_hardening.py, test_pydantic_ai_runner.py) already cover overlapping cases, risking either duplication or silent gaps.
findings.location: What section — 'A parametrized test file…'; Scope section

findings.finding: The Scope names 'UsageLimitExceeded (request_limit=50)' as a failure class to inject, but the runner derives its actual request_limit as `max(1, math.ceil(eff_max_iter / 2))` from `cfg.max_iterations` (runner.py:310) — it never uses pydantic-ai's default 50. A looping FunctionModel injected via model_override will trip the runner's computed limit (e.g. 3 for max_iterations=5), not 50. Asserting 'request_limit=50' produces either a wrong fixture configuration or an assertion against a value that is never the operative limit, breaking the mechanism the plan intends to pin.
findings.location: Scope section — 'UsageLimitExceeded (request_limit=50)' failure class
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet, change 'UsageLimitExceeded (request_limit=50)' to 'UsageLimitExceeded (request_limit derived from cfg.max_iterations, e.g. max_iterations=5 → request_limit=3)' and require the fixture to set `max_iterations` explicitly so the assertion is `calls["n"] <= ceil(max_iterations/2)`.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a looping model reaches the default `request_limit=50`' as the `UsageLimitExceeded` trigger for the liveness scenario, but the runner at runtime computes `req_limit = max(1, math.ceil(eff_max_iter / 2))` from `cfg.max_iterations` and explicitly avoids pydantic-ai's default of 50 (runner.py:292, 310). A `FunctionModel` that loops will trip the derived limit (e.g., 12–25 depending on config), not 50.
findings.location: Scope section — 'looping model reaches the default request_limit=50' / runner.py:290-314
findings.suggested_fix: Set `LLMConfig(max_iterations=4)` in the looping test; the runner will set `request_limit=2`; assert `UsageLimitExceeded` fires after 2 model calls. Document `request_limit=50` only if you also set `max_iterations=100` explicitly.

findings.finding: The Scope section describes a liveness scenario where 'a looping model reaches the default `request_limit=50`', but today's runner explicitly overrides that default: it computes `req_limit = ceil(max_iterations/2)` (runner.py:310) and passes that to `UsageLimits(request_limit=req_limit)`, so the pydantic-ai default of 50 is never used. A test that relies on the agent running 50 iterations before tripping `UsageLimitExceeded` will instead trip at `ceil(max_iterations/2)`, producing an incorrect baseline pin.
findings.location: Scope section — 'a looping model reaches the default `request_limit=50`'
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the approach in test_runner_pydantic_budget.py: `cfg = replace(LLMConfig.from_env(), max_iterations=5)` so `req_limit=3`, then assert the FunctionModel loop trips `LLMRunnerError` after ≤3 model calls.

findings.finding: The failure matrix labels a looping-model scenario `UsageLimitExceeded (request_limit=50)`, but the runner at runner.py:310 explicitly computes `req_limit = max(1, math.ceil(eff_max_iter / 2))` — it never uses pydantic-ai's default of 50. The operative limit for a looping FunctionModel via model_override is the derived value (e.g. `ceil(DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS/2)`), not 50. The plan therefore mis-specifies the decision protocol the test must configure against.
findings.location: Scope section — failure matrix: '`UsageLimitExceeded (request_limit=50)`'
findings.suggested_fix: Label the scenario 'UsageLimitExceeded (request_limit=ceil(max_iterations/2))' and configure the test with an explicit `max_iterations` (e.g. 4) so the expected limit (2) is stated in the test comment.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
